key_points: The Human Brain is divided into three main regions: Forebrain, Midbrain, and Hindbrain, each specialized for different functions.
Coordination in plants is achieved through chemical means rather than a nervous system, using substances called Phytohormones.
Plant movements are categorized into Tropic movements (directional growth) and Nastic movements (non-directional, immediate responses).
The Peripheral Nervous System (PNS) facilitates communication between the Central Nervous System and the rest of the body.
The Forebrain (Cerebrum) is the main thinking part of the brain and controls voluntary actions, memory, and sensory interpretations.
examples: Brain Functions: Medulla (Hindbrain) controls blood pressure and salivation; Cerebellum (Hindbrain) controls balance and posture.
Phototropism: Shoots growing toward light; Geotropism: Roots growing downward toward gravity.
Hydrotropism: Growth of roots toward water; Chemotropism: Growth of pollen tubes toward ovules.
Plant Hormones: Auxins promote cell elongation; Gibberellins aid in stem growth; Cytokinins promote cell division.
Growth Inhibitor: Abscisic acid causes wilting of leaves and inhibits growth.
formulas: Brain Protection: $	ext{Cranium (Bony Box)} + 	ext{Cerebrospinal Fluid (Shock Absorber)}$
Auxin Action: $	ext{Light from one side} ightarrow 	ext{Auxin diffuses to shady side} ightarrow 	ext{Cells grow longer on shady side} ightarrow 	ext{Stem bends to light}$
exam_tips: Don't confuse the Cerebrum (Forebrain - thinking/intelligence) with the Cerebellum (Hindbrain - balance/walking).
Remember that Cytokinins are present in greater concentrations in areas of rapid cell division, like fruits and seeds.
Note that the Midbrain and Hindbrain (Medulla) control most of the involuntary actions of the body.
Differentiate between 'Tropic' (growth-dependent) and 'Nastic' (growth-independent) movements in plants.
